pages.text: Laboratory evaluation of HMA with high density polyethylene as a modiﬁer Moatasim Attaelmanan a,⇑, Cheng Pei Feng b, Al-Hadidy AI c a Northeast Forestry University (NEFU), Harbin, PR China b Civil Engineering Dept., North East Forestry University (NEFU), Harbin, PR China c Civil Engineering Dept., Mosul University, Mosul, Iraq article info Article history: Received 11 August 2010 Received in revised form 16 November 2010 Accepted 20 December 2010 Available online 8 January 2011 Keywords: High density polyethylene Asphalt mixtures Performance tests abstract This paper investigates the viability of using high density polyethylene (HDPE) as a modiﬁer for asphalt paving materials. Different ratios of HDPE by weight of asphalt were blended with 80/100 paving grade asphalt. Unmodiﬁed and modiﬁed asphalt binders were subjected to physicochemical and homogeneity tests. The performance tests including, Marshall Stability, Marshall Quotient (MQ), tensile strength, ten- sile strength ratio, ﬂexural strength and resilient modulus were carried out on unmodiﬁed and modiﬁed hot asphalt mixtures. The analyses of test results show that the performance of HDPE-modiﬁed asphalt mixtures are better than conventional mixtures. The moisture susceptibility and temperature susceptibil- ity can be reduced by the inclusion of HDPE in the asphalt mixture. A HDPE content of 5% by weight of asphalt is recommended for the improvement of the performance of asphalt concrete mixtures similar to that investigated in this study. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction One of the main components of hot mix asphalt (HMA) is as- phalt cement binder. Asphalt cement is dark brown-to-black ce- ment-like material obtained mainly by petroleum reﬁning. Asphalt is used primarily construction of roads and as a major component in rooﬁng materials due to its remarkable binding and waterprooﬁng properties. Asphalts are highly complex and not well characterized materi- als containing saturated and unsaturated aliphatic and aromatic compounds with molecular weights from 500 to 2000 and carbon numbers higher than C 25 [1,2]. Asphalt typically contains about: 80% carbon; 10% hydrogen; up to 6% sulfur; 6.5% asphaltenes; 0.51% small amounts of oxygen and nitrogen; and metals such as iron 33 ppm, nickel 18 ppm, vanadium 39 ppm, copper <1 ppm [1]. The compounds are classiﬁed as asphaltenes or maltenes according to their solubility in hexane or heptane [3]. Asphaltenes are high-molecular weight species that are insoluble in these sol- vents, whereas maltenes have lower molecular weights and are soluble. Asphalts normally contain between 5% and 25% by weight of asphaltenes and may be regarded as colloids of asphaltene mi- celles dispersed in maltenes. Asphalt has long been classiﬁed as gel or sol-type. Gel-type as- phalts usually exhibit pronounced non-Newtonian behavior, whereas sol-type asphalts are more Newtonian. Gel-type asphalts generally contain large amounts of asphaltenes, and sol-type as- phalts are characterized by low asphaltene content. Using the clas- sical asphalt science terminology, sol-type asphalts are more compatible, while gel-type asphalts are less compatible [4]. Less compatible (gel-type) asphalt is known to be highly susceptible to oxidative age hardening. While highly compatible (sol-type) as- phalt is not susceptible to oxidative age hardening. The behavior of asphalt cement in service is governed by their initial engineering properties as well as

whereas the static indirect tensile strength values were found to be much higher. They also investigated the future recyclability of the Plastiphalt. It was found that the mechanical properties of the recycled mix to be equal to that of the original Plastiphalt and bet- ter than the control mix. Hee et al. [21] described the results of laboratory and full-scale performance tests for a high durability asphalt binder (HDAB) and high durability asphalt mixture (HDAM) applicable to the wearing course in a bridge deck system. The HDAB was developed using a SBS-modiﬁer and hydrocarbon for improving construction work- ability and resistance to fatigue cracking. Various binder tests were conducted on the HDAB, and test results showed that the resistance to fatigue and low temperature cracking of the HDAB increased sig- niﬁcantly compared to that of the PG 64-22 and PG 76-22 binders. For the mixtures, the fatigue test results showed that the HDAM has a three times longer fatigue life than the SBS-modiﬁed asphalt mix- ture. It was also found that the HDAM has a higher resistance against moisture-induced damage. Results from full-scale acceler- ated testing on the bridge deck pavement system proved that the HDAM can signiﬁcantly improve pavement performance. Al-Hadidy and Tan [22] investigates the potential use of pyroli- sis LDPE as a modiﬁer for asphalt paving materials. Five different blends including conventional mix were subjected to binder test- ing such as rheological tests, as well as to some other tests related to the homogeneity of the system. Research results indicate that modiﬁed binders showed higher softening point, keeping the val- ues of ductility at minimum range of speciﬁcation of (100 + cm), and caused a reduction in percentage loss of weight due to heat and air (i.e. increase durability of original asphalt). Al-Hadidy and Tan [23] investigates the beneﬁts of modifying the gel asphalt in ﬂexible pavement.50/60 penetration grade as- phalt cement and four proportion of pyrolisis PP were selected. Unmodiﬁed and modiﬁed asphalt binders were subjected to rheo- logical and homogeneity tests. The analyses of test results show that the performance of PP-modiﬁed asphalt mixtures are better when compared to conventional mixtures. The temperature sus- ceptibility can be reduced by the inclusion of PP in the asphalt mix- ture. A PP content of 5% by weight of asphalt is recommended for the improvement of the performance of asphalt cement. However, this study is an attempt to satisfy the following objectives: 1. Possibility of using American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) [24] 13 mm dense HMA gradation on Chinese paving materials. 2. To determine the physiochemical and homogeneity properties of HDPE-modiﬁed asphalt binder (HDPEMAB) and compare the results with 80/100 grade asphalt cement. 3. To evaluate temperature susceptibility of HDPEMAB and com- pare the results with asphalt cement. 4. To investigate the moisture and temperature susceptibility of HDPE-modiﬁed HMA (HDPEMHMA) mixtures. 5. To evaluate the low temperature performance of HDPEMHMA mixtures; and 6. To determine the resilient modulus of HDPEMHMA mixtures from the repeated load indirect tensile test and compare the results with the control mixtures. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Asphalt cement, HDPE and aggregate The asphalt cement used in this investigation was obtained from one asphalt plant in Heilongjiang province. The asphalt was 80/100-penetration grade and has bee

The MR represents the ratio of an applied stress to the recoverable strain that takes place after the applied stress has been removed. The MR was determined from tests on cylindrical specimens for each mixture at designed asphalt contents in the indirect tension mode. About 15% of the indirect tensile strength of each mixture was applied on the vertical diameter for conventional and HDPE-modiﬁed specimens. For the repeated load indirect tensile test, Marshall specimens prepared with a particular binder were subjected to repeated compressive loads in equipment fab- ricated for this purpose. The frequency of load application used was 1 Hz, with a load duration of 0.1 s to represent ﬁeld conditions and a resting period of 0.9 s. The tests were conducted at 25 /C176C; the test procedure adopted was as per ASTM 4123 [16]. Constant test temperature was maintained using an environmental air chamber. Each specimen was placed inside the chamber at the set temperature for 3 h before testing. For each of the ﬁve kinds of mixtures evaluated, three sam- ples were made and took their averages to represent the MR for each mixture. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Rheological tests The rheological properties of HDPE–asphalt binders were eval- uated and the results are presented in Table 3. The results indicate that HDPE is effective in improving the rheological properties of as- phalt cement. Examining Table 3, it can be seen that HDPE is keeping the duc- tility values of 100 + up to 5% which indicates that HDPE is com- pletely dissolved in asphalt cement and the uniformity test insure this result. The softening point of virgin asphalt was raised by 69% at 5%wt. HDPE, whereas, the penetration was decreased by 35% with the addition of 5% HDPE, which indicates improvements in resistance to deformation. 3.2. Temperature susceptibility The penetration index relationship was used to investigate the inﬂuence of HDPE addition on temperature susceptibility of as- phalt cement using the following Eq. (1) mentioned by Yang [26]: Penetration index ðP:I:Þ¼½ ð 20 /C0 500AÞ=ð1 þ 50AÞ/C138 ð 1Þ A ¼½ ðlog pen:@T /C0 log 800Þ=ðT /C0 TR&BÞ/C138 where T is the testing temperature, /C176C. TR2B is the ring and ball soft- ening point, /C176C. Fig. 2 illustrates the relationship between the penetration index and HDPE contents. A higher value of P.I. indicates the lower tem- perature susceptibility of binder. From Fig. 2 , it can be observed that the values of P.I. for virgin and 5% HDPETMAB samples are /C0 2.353 and 3.155, respectively. This signiﬁcant fact demonstrates that HDPE will lead to make this binder less susceptible to temper- ature and more favorable for hot climates. 3.3. Durability test Fig. 3 shows that the percentage loss of HDPE binders decreases as the HDPE content increases. This is related to that HDPE occu- pied a space of total mix and causes reduction in asphalt volume, which leads to a decrease in loss by dehydrogenation and oxidation of asphalt in the mix (i.e. durability increased slightly with the addition of HDPE content in the mix). 3.4. Uniformity test The uniformity between HDPE and asphalt was studied by pass- ing the binder at 170 /C176C through an ASTM 100 sieve. It was found that the binder thus prepared can be stored for future use [16]. 1001010.10.01 Seive size (mm) % passing Lower limit Upper limit Middle limit 0 20 40 60 80 100 120 Fig. 1. 3.5. Engineering properties of HMA mixtures 3.5.1. Statistical considerations Results of the Marshall, indirect tensile, tensile strength ratio, ﬂexural strength and resilient modulus tests were statistically ana- lyzed with a 5% level of signiﬁcance. For these comparisons, it should be noted that all specimens were produced at optimum as- phalt content. 3.5.2. Marshall properties Three specimens of unmodiﬁed and modiﬁed mixtures were prepared at the optimum bitumen content of 5.2%, to determine the Marshall properties. Specimens were placed in water bath at 60 /C176C for 30 min and then loaded at a ratio of 50.8 mm/min and the stability and ﬂow values were recorded. Marshall test results were summarized in Fig. 4. From Fig. 4, it can be seen that the addi- tion of 5% HDPE raises the Marshall Stability of control mix by 13%, whereas decreases in ﬂow value was observed with the addition of these modiﬁers. As indicated in Fig. 4 , all HDPE contents satisfy the speciﬁed limits of 3–5% air voids mentioned by ASTM speciﬁcation [24] for HMA 13 mm. From the sited results in Fig. 4d it was found that the MQ value increased by 55% at 5% HDPE content. modiﬁed bituminous binders provide better resistance against per- manent deformations due to their high stability and high MQ and indicate that HDPE–HMA mixtures could be use in military airﬁeld pavements where stiff asphalt mixture and low asphalt content are required. 3.5.3. Moisture susceptibility Tensile strength for HDPE–asphalt mixtures are given in Fig. 5 . The results indicate that tensile strength and tensile strength ratio increased for both testing temperatures (i.e. increase the adhesion between aggregate and bitumen, which leads to a decrease in the stripping of HMA). Tensile strength for HDPE-modiﬁed asphalt mixture is slightly higher than for conventional mixtures. Test re- sults indicated that the TSR values were 79.82% and 94.81%, respectively, for bitumen and modiﬁed bitumen containing 5%HDPE using Heilongjiang aggregate. It can be seen that the HDPE-modiﬁed bitumen improves the resistance to moisture sus- ceptibility of the asphalt mixtures. 3.5.4. Resilient modulus The MR test results are presented in Fig. 6. From Fig. 6, it can be observed that the MR values for conventional and 5% HDPE-modi- ﬁed asphalt samples are 1515 MPa and 2410 MPa, respectively. The percentage increase in the average MR is found to be compar- atively signiﬁcant. The study showed that the percentage increase in MR value with the addition of 5% HDPE in asphalt was found to be 59%. 3.5.5. Low temperature performance It is commonly accepted that at low temperature, bituminous materials exhibit elastic behavior. For an elastic material, the mod- ulus is deﬁned as the ratio of stress/strain. For bituminous materi- als, this is deﬁned as stiffness modulus [27] which, in the case of ﬂexural testing, is called the ﬂexural stiffness. The ﬂexural stiffness is determined as follows: St ¼½ ðP /C2 LÞ=ðb /C2 d 2 Þ/C138 ð 2Þ where St is the stiffness modulus, kN/mm2. P is load at break, kN. L is beam span, mm. b is av. beam width, mm. d is av. beam depth, mm. St is a measure of the stiffness of the material. In addition, another parameter may be determined, namely ﬂexural modulus of the material is determined by: Sr ¼½ ðP /C2 L3Þ=ð6 /C2 b 2 /C2 d 2 /C2 DÞ/C138 ð 3Þ where Sr is ﬂexural modulus (modulus of rupture), kN/mm 2. D is deﬂection at the beam center, mm; and the other variables as de- ﬁned above. Fig. 7 summarizes test results for the conventional and 5% HDPE-modiﬁed asphalt concrete mixtures. The testing was con- ducted at 0 and /C0 10 /C176C. Stiffness and modulus of rupture for HDPE-modiﬁed asphalt mixtures is slightly higher than for conven- tional asphalt mixtures because the viscosity of HDPE-modiﬁed as- phalt is higher than that of conventional asphalt. Test results indicated that the inclusion of HDPE content in asphalt mixtures reduces the cracking potential at low temperatures. 01234567 % HDPE Resilient Modulus (Mpa) 0 500 1000 1500 2000 2500 3000 Fig. 6. Attaelmanan et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 2764–2770 2769

4. Conclusions Based on the testing and analysis presented herein, the conclu- sions of the study are summarized as follows: (A) A review of the standard binder testing and grading results indicated the following: 1. Penetration at 25 /C176C will generally decrease as HDPE con- tent increases, which indicates an improved shear resis- tance in medium to high temperatures. 2. The addition of HDPE kept the ductility values at a mini- mum range of ASTM speciﬁcations of 100+ cm up to 5% HDPE content. 3. Softening point tend to increase with the addition of HDPE content, which indicates improvement in resistance to deformation. 4. Penetration index values indicated that HDPE reduced the temperature susceptibility of asphalt. 5. The ductility and uniformity test results indicated that HDPE and asphalt are compatible to an extent; and 6. The percentage loss of air and heat decreases with the addition of HDPE (i.e. the resistance of asphalt to the action of temperature and temperature changes and the action of heat increases). (B) A review of the drain down, Marshall, indirect tensile strength, ﬂexural strength and resilient modulus results indicated the following: 1. Marshall results indicated that 5% HDPE raised the MQ of control mix by 55%. It can be said that HDPE-modiﬁed bituminous binders provide better resistance against per- manent deformations due to their high stability and high MQ and indicate that HDPEMHMA mixtures could be use in military airﬁeld pavements where stiff asphalt mixture and low asphalt content are required. 2. The tensile strength ratios for the mixtures containing the HDPE were greater than 85%. This indicates that this type of modiﬁer does not cause the mixture to weaken when exposed to moisture. 3. The inclusion of HDPE increases the stiffness and modulus of rupture values of the asphalt mixtures at low temper- ature ( /C0 10 /C176C) which may reduce the cracking potential of pavements at low temperatures. HDPE-modiﬁed asphalt mixtures were found to have lesser strain values compared to conventional asphalt concrete mixtures. 4. The use of HDPE in asphalt concrete mixtures caused an increase in resilient modulus values at high (25 /C176C) tem- peratures; and 5. Results indicated that ﬂexible pavement with high perfor- mance, durability and more economic can be obtained with 5% HDPE. References [1] Klimisch HJ, Andreae M, Tillman U. J Colloid Interface Sci 2004;280:366–73. [8] Reynaldo R, Bjorn B, Christos Drakos. Guidelines for use of modiﬁed binders. Final re

Examples of polymers commonly used in asphalt mixtures and a few of their advantages and disadvantages are presented below [3]: /C15 SBS (radial and linear) – good fatigue resistance, high creep rate, but oxidation susceptibility. /C15 PE – high thermal extension but low stiffness. /C15 SBR – very good aging and weather resistance but low tear strength, low ozone resistance, oxidation susceptibility. /C15 Polybutadiene (PB) – excellent wear resistance, impact resil- ience, but low strength. /C15 EVA – storage stability, reducing binder run-off, high tempera- ture viscosity, but may be susceptible to crosslink and low creep resistance. EVA is very good for low temperature zones but pre- sents differential cooling areas along the chain and tearing phe- nomena is commonly observed. Also due to reduced initial stiffness of the material ‘‘chipping loss’’ is often experienced. /C15 Ethylene methyl acrylate copolymer (EMA). /C15 PP – good chemical resistance, good fatigue resistance but oxi- dative degradation, high mould shrinkage and thermal expan- sion was observed /C15 Epoxies and urethanes, tire rubber (Crumb) – polymers can be classiﬁed as: elastomeric, with a high level of elastic recovery (i.e. SBS, SBR, Crumb rubber); and plastomeric, with high stiffness and deformation resistance (i.e. EVA, EMA, Urethanes, and PP). The properties of asphalt-polymer blends depend on the con- centrations and the type of polymer used. The polymer is usually loaded in concentrations of about 4–6% by weight with respect to the asphalt [7]. Higher concentrations of polymers are consid- ered to be economically less viable and also may cause other prob- lems related to the material properties. Desirable characteristics of polymer modiﬁed asphalt binders (PMABs) include greater elastic recovery, a higher softening point, greater viscosity, greater cohe- sive strength and greater ductility [3,8–10]. In addition, the use of polymers provides better drainage suppression, as well as it pro- vides better cost beneﬁts due to the avoidance of using ﬁbrous materials in stone matrix asphalt (SMA) mixtures. Processes of asphalt modiﬁcation involving natural and syn- thetic polymers were patented as early as 1843. Test projects were underway in Europe in the 1930s, and neoprene latex began to be used in North America in the 1950s. In the late 1970s, Europe was ahead of the United States in the use of modiﬁed asphalts because the European use of contractors, who provided warranties, moti- vated a greater interest in decreased life cycle costs, even at higher initial costs. The high preliminary expenses for PMA limited its use in the US. In the mid-1980s, newer polymers were developed and European technologies began to be used in the US. At the same time, the prevalence of a long-term economic outlook in the coun- try increased [9]. In Australia, the National Asphalt Speciﬁcation in- cludes guides and speciﬁcations regarding PMABs [11]. The United States Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) has developed a life cycle cost analysis approach, which can be used to evaluate the life cycle costs of pavement containing asphalt rubber binders as well as other treatments. The ﬁndings indicated that as- phalt rubber is cost effective as it is used, for example, in Arizona and California, although the estimated life of the pavement is based on interviews and engineering judgment, and can be reﬁned as the pavement ages and long-term ﬁeld performance is included in the model [12].
